questions.prompt: Why is MCP especially valuable for assistant products vs. one-off agents?

choices.text: It's faster than direct API calls.

Assistants typically need more tools, last longer, ship on multiple surfaces, and share integrations with other products in the same org. MCP turns those integrations from per-product code into shared infrastructure — build once, reuse across all assistants. The savings compound as the assistant lineup grows.
It's required by major LLM providers.

questions.explanation: MCP reduces integration cost on the second, third, and tenth assistant product. For one-off agents the upfront cost might match a custom integration; for assistant suites, MCP pays back fast through reuse.

questions.prompt: You're considering connecting your assistant to a community MCP filesystem server with broad scope. Right approach?

choices.text: Connect it — community servers are pre-vetted.

Sandbox first. Review the server's code; constrain its filesystem scope to a specific directory; restrict network access; pin the version. MCP standardizes the integration layer, not the safety controls — community servers can have security issues, and a successful prompt injection through one can have wide blast radius.

questions.explanation: MCP doesn't replace the safety patterns from earlier modules — it standardizes integration but the assistant still needs sandboxing, approval gates, and audit logs. Treat community MCP servers like any other third-party dependency: review, sandbox, version-pin, monitor.
